title: Weak Learners to Strong Learners
concept: Boosting builds powerful ensembles by combining many 'weak learners' — simple models like shallow trees (stumps) that perform just slightly better than random guessing.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main structural difference between bagging and boosting?

options.text: Bagging models train in parallel; boosting models train sequentially.
Bagging uses decision trees; boosting only uses linear regression.
Bagging is unsupervised; boosting is supervised.

exercises.explain: Correct! Sequential vs. parallel training is the core difference.

cards.title: The committee of average students
cards.body: If you need to translate an ancient text, would you hire one average student? No. But what if you hired 50 average students, where one knows grammar, one knows history, one knows verbs, and one knows vocabulary? By pooling their shallow knowledge, they can collectively write a translation that matches a master scholar. This is the weak-learner strategy.

cards.title: Weak Learners and Decision Stumps
cards.body: A weak learner is a model that performs poorly on its own, but is slightly better than random guessing (accuracy > 50%). In boosting, the most common weak learner is a **decision stump** — a decision tree with a max depth of exactly **1** (only one question split).

cards.title: A single compass check
cards.body: A decision stump is like a compass that only tells you 'North' or 'South'. It's not a full map, and it won't get you to your exact street. But if you combine it with 50 other simple compass checks ('East/West', 'Uphill/Downhill', 'Near water/Far from water'), you can map your location perfectly.

cards.title: A forest of stumps
cards.body: To classify spam, standard trees grow deep questions. In boosting, we train 100 decision stumps. Stump 1: 'Does it contain free?'. Stump 2: 'Is capitalization high?'. Stump 3: 'Is the sender in contacts?'. Individually, each stump is weak. Combined, their weighted votes form a highly accurate classifier.

cards.title: Deep trees are bad for boosting
cards.body: You might think: 'If we boost deep trees, won't it be even more accurate?' In reality, boosting deep trees leads to rapid overfitting. Because boosting builds on errors, deep base trees memorize noise immediately. We need weak, simple models (like stumps) as the bricks to build a stable house.

cards.title: Decision Tree vs. Decision Stump
cards.body: Comparing a standard tree to a weak learner stump:

left.text:
Multiple question levels (depth > 3).
Finds complex rules on its own.
Can overfit if not restricted.

right.text:
Only ONE split level (depth = 1).
Only asks one yes/no question.
Cannot overfit on its own.

exercises.prompt: What is a decision stump in machine learning?

options.text: A decision tree with a max depth of exactly 1.
A tree that has had all its branches pruned.
A model that only predicts the average value.
A support vector machine using a linear kernel.

exercises.explain: Correct! A decision stump is the simplest tree, splitting only once on a single feature.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Boosting works best when its base models are deep, complex decision trees that fit the training data perfectly.
exercises.explain: False! Deep trees overfit quickly in boosting. Boosting requires simple, weak learners (like stumps) to remain generalizable.

exercises.sentence: A weak learner must perform at least slightly better than random ___.
exercises.explain: A weak learner must have an accuracy rate greater than 50% (random guess chance in binary classification).

exercises.prompt: Which of these models is a decision stump?

options.text: If salary > $80k -> Classify: High Spend; else Classify: Low Spend.
If salary > $80k AND age < 30 -> Classify: High Spend.
A model predicting salary based on age using linear regression.

exercises.explain: Correct! A single conditional check resulting in classification is a decision stump.

exercises.prompt: Why are weak learners combined in boosting rather than just training one highly accurate model?

options.text: Individual weak learners have high bias but low variance; combining them sequentially reduces bias, resulting in a highly accurate model that resists overfitting.
Weak learners require no training data at all.
Weak learners are unsupervised, making them faster to evaluate.
One strong model cannot handle numerical data.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Weak learners (like stumps) are too simple to overfit (low variance), but make many mistakes (high bias). By combining them sequentially, each new stump corrects the bias of the previous ones. The final ensemble achieves low bias and low variance. Next, let's see how AdaBoost adjusts weights to target these mistakes!
